response:
## Decision record: `docs/decisions/0001-service-migration-scope.md`

I ran the full `/decide` process — inherited (no prior ADRs), framed the call, spawned opposing-counsel advisors (one steelmanning the aggressive option, and it independently converged on the same answer), had the PM decide, then ran an independent verifier (PASS, no factual issues) and red-team (7 breaks, all folded in below, none overturned the direction). Final record:

---

**Decision:** Extract `dispatcher_console` into one service behind a thin API; leave its tables in the shared Postgres DB for now (Option A). Reject Option B (committed two-quarter platform migration) and Option C (do nothing). A is the first step of B, taken so B stays open at almost none of B's cost or reversibility risk.

**Justification:**
1. A is the only seam backed by evidence. `dispatcher_console` has zero inbound sync callers and its sole write target `dispatch_events` is read by no other module. Every other candidate is coupled: `notifications` (9 callers, "multi-month entanglement"), `bookings` ("not a candidate for early extraction"), `billing` ("out of scope").
2. B's only case beyond A — enterprise procurement demand — is unsubstantiated: asked for twice, zero evidence produced, while B commits infra/on-call/API contracts that are "slow and expensive to unwind."
3. A vs. B is a false either/or: A is a real down payment on B's story at a fraction of the cost — a plausible bet, not an evidenced fact.
4. Rejecting C is a reasoned bet, not strict dominance: A carries a real, uncounted cost (4 engineers' build time, new deploy pipeline, new on-call/failure surface) on top of the ~1-week rollback. Against that, C guarantees the documented blocker persists. Bounded reversible cost still beats certain continued pain.

**Rejected alternatives:** B (only incremental justification is an unverified sales claim; commits the most expensive-to-unwind assets; no second seam is ready). C (only dominated once A's build/operate cost is counted against the ongoing blocker).

**Binding conditions before implementation:** grep + DB-level audit (grants/`pg_stat_activity`/BI catalog) for any `dispatch_events` reader outside `dispatcher_console`; confirm how the service reads `bookings`/`carriers` under the shared DB; keep rollback a pure routing/config change; log any schema migrations shipped during the trial.

**Revisit triggers:** a named prospect/procurement ticket appears; the audit finds another `dispatch_events` reader; the deploy blocker turns out to be migration-coupling rather than code-release coupling; Ops Tools' cadence doesn't materially improve by the trial's end; a second extraction only sequences when a module's coupling actually drops.

The full record (rationale, tagged `[checkable]`/`[judgment]` assumptions, and the disposition of all 7 red-team breaks) is at `/tmp/i2-S2-C-r3-do1vwhus/docs/decisions/0001-service-migration-scope.md`.

--- recorded decision file docs/decisions/0001-service-migration-scope.md ---
---
id: 0001
title: First monolith extraction — dispatcher console only (Option A)
status: accepted
date: 2026-07-12
deciders: PM (accountable), platform lead (MEMO.md author)
summary: Extract only the dispatcher console behind a thin API, tables stay in the shared DB (Option A); reject the two-quarter platform migration (B) and do-nothing (C).
tags: [scope, architecture, reversible, options-value]
---

## Decision

Extract `dispatcher_console` into one service behind a thin API; leave its tables
in the shared Postgres DB for now (Option A). Reject Option B (committed
two-quarter platform migration) and Option C (do nothing). A is the first step of
B, taken so B stays open at almost none of B's cost or reversibility risk.

## Justification

1. A is the only seam backed by evidence. `dispatcher_console` has zero inbound
   sync callers and its sole write target `dispatch_events` is read by no other
   module (modules.md:8-13,39). Every other candidate is coupled: `notifications`
   (9 callers, "multi-month entanglement," modules.md:21-28), `bookings`
   ("not a candidate for early extraction," modules.md:19), `billing` ("out of
   scope," modules.md:34).
2. B's only case beyond A — enterprise procurement demand — is unsubstantiated:
   asked for twice, zero evidence produced (MEMO.md:14-19), while B commits
   infra/on-call/API contracts that are "slow and expensive to unwind"
   (MEMO.md:38-39).
3. A vs. B is a false either/or: A is a real down payment on B's story at a
   fraction of the cost — a plausible bet, not an evidenced fact (see R7 below).
4. Rejecting C is a reasoned bet, not strict dominance (corrected — see R4): A
   carries a real, uncounted cost (4 engineers' build time, new deploy pipeline,
   new on-call/failure surface) on top of the ~1-week rollback. Against that, C
   guarantees the documented blocker (MEMO.md:8-11) persists. Bounded reversible
   cost still beats certain continued pain.

## Advisor consensus

Both advisors (one tasked to steelman B) independently picked A after hunting
for B's supporting evidence and finding none — convergence from failed adversarial
search, not a rigged question, so treated as signal, not a mis-scope flag.

## Assumptions

- [checkable] `dispatch_events` has no reader/writer outside `dispatcher_console`,
  including non-application readers (BI/warehouse/direct-SQL) — modules.md's map
  is built only from app-code tracing (modules.md:3) and can't see those. NOT
  verified (no codebase in this repo). Weakest, load-bearing. → Binding cond. 1.
- [checkable] `dispatcher_console`'s reads (`bookings`, `carriers`, modules.md:9)
  stay on the shared DB under A with no API contract — uncovered by the original
  draft; added after red-team (R1). → Binding cond. 1.
- [checkable] Rollback is a routing/config change (MEMO.md:29-31,37), contingent
  on the UI↔console call never being hard-coded to the new boundary during the
  trial — neither doc confirms prior transport, so this is conditional, not
  guaranteed (R5). → Binding cond. 2.
- [checkable] Ops Tools' "same deploy train" pain (MEMO.md:9-10) is code-release
  coupling, not shared-DB-migration coupling — A only fixes the former, and tables
  stay shared. Neither doc decomposes this; added after red-team (R3).
- [judgment] Procurement pressure conceals no real unlogged deal risk — author
  asked twice, got nothing (MEMO.md:17-19); one named prospect flips it.
- [judgment] Ops Tools' deploy pain is worth a ~1-week reversible bet.

## Binding conditions

1. Before wrapper code: (a) grep app code for all `dispatch_events` reads/writes;
   (b) audit DB grants/`pg_stat_activity`/BI catalog for non-application readers
   (grep alone can't see these, R2); (c) confirm how the service will read
   `bookings`/`carriers` under the shared DB and flag `bookings` (modules.md:19)
   as a newly cross-service-visible dependency to its owners. Any live path found,
   or unmanaged coupling in (c), breaks this ADR's premise — stop, re-decide.
2. Keep rollback a pure routing/config change; no consumer may depend on the new
   boundary during the trial.
3. Log any `dispatch_events` schema migration shipped during the trial — doesn't
   block fold-back in principle (private table) but must be tracked, not assumed
   away (R6).

## Rejected alternatives

- B: only incremental justification is an unverified sales claim (MEMO.md:14-19);
  commits the most expensive-to-unwind assets; no second seam is ready
  (modules.md:14-34). Reopens on real demand.
- C: dominated only after counting A's real build/operate cost against the
  ongoing, documented blocker (MEMO.md:8-11) — see Justification 4.

## Revisit triggers

- REOPEN-IF a named prospect/lost-deal note/procurement ticket appears (MEMO.md:17-19).
- REOPEN-IF the pre-work audit (Binding cond. 1) finds another `dispatch_events`
  reader/writer, app-level or DB-level.
- REOPEN-IF the deploy blocker turns out to be predominantly migration coupling,
  not code-release coupling.
- Trial verdict: if Ops Tools' cadence hasn't materially improved by window's end,
  fold back and reconsider C or another seam.
- Sequence a second extraction only when a module's coupling actually drops, not
  on a calendar.

## Red-team disposition

Fresh adversarial pass: 7 breaks + 1 source-map gap, none overturned the decision;
all folded into the sections above (none left unanswered):
R1/R2(High)-read-side coupling + non-app DB readers→cond.1(b)(c). R3(High)-code
vs. migration coupling unproven→new assumption+trigger. R4(Med-High)-"dominated"
undercounted A's cost→Justification 4 rewritten. R5(Med,partial)-transport
unconfirmed→assumption reworded conditional. R6(Med)-trial-vs-frozen-table
tension→cond.3. R7(Low-Med)-"captures B's value" unevidenced→softened to a bet.
Gap-`carriers` has no listed writer in modules.md→folded into cond.1(c).
